mainTopic: חזרה במכירת חייטה · המוכר שדה לחבירו ונמצאת שאינה שלו
overview: (1) The discussion concludes the previous topic regarding a buyer's ability to retract from a purchase of a beast before taking possession. (2) An extensive debate between Rav and Shmuel regarding whether a buyer of land that turns out not to belong to the seller retains the improvements (שבח) and the principal (מעות), including various scenarios of theft, debt, and the nature of the payment. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The discussion concludes the previous topic regarding a buyer's ability to retract from a purchase of a beast before taking possession.

sugyaBoundaries.topic: המוכר שדה לחבירו ונמצאת שאינה שלו
sugyaBoundaries.gist: An extensive debate between Rav and Shmuel regarding whether a buyer of land that turns out not to belong to the seller retains the improvements (שבח) and the principal (מעות), including various scenarios of theft, debt, and the nature of the payment. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: The rule of חֲזָרָה before and after חֲזָקָה
steps.stepSummary: The Mishnah establishes that a buyer can retract from a purchase of a beast before taking possession, but cannot retract after taking possession.
steps.whatsHappening: The Mishnah establishes the basic halachic rule for retracting from a sale of a beast. It distinguishes between the period before and after the buyer takes possession.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Before חֲזָקָה, the buyer may retract; after חֲזָקָה, retraction is forbidden. The rationale is that once possession is taken, the seller assumes the buyer accepted the beast as-is, including any hidden defects.
steps.whatToRemember: The pivotal moment is חֲזָקָה. Before it, the sale is not final; after it, it is final.
steps.confusionAlert: The Mishnah does not define חֲזָקָה here; the Gemara will ask what constitutes חֲזָקָה.
steps.whyThisMatters: This is the foundational principle for all sales of movable property: possession finalizes the transaction.

phrases.english: He can return it,
From the time he took possession of it –
He cannot return it.

phrases.aramaic: דְּאָמַר לֵיהּ: חַיְיתָא דְקִטְרֵי סְבַרְתְּ וְקַבֵּלְתְּ.
phrases.english: For he said to him: 'You thought it was a living creature and you accepted…
From when was it a possession?
Even if he threshed in Egypt.

steps.triggerLanguage: יָכוֹל לַחֲזוֹר בּוֹ, מִשֶּׁהֶחְזִיק בָּהּ – אֵינוֹ יָכוֹל לַחֲזוֹר בּוֹ.

rashi.hebrew: יכול לחזור בו - ובשלא נתן מעות:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the ability to retract before חֲזָקָה applies specifically when the buyer has not yet paid the purchase price.

rashi.hebrew: אינו יכול לחזור בו - ואפילו לא נתן מעות שהקרקע נקנית בחזקה והמעות על זה מלוה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that after חֲזָקָה, retraction is forbidden even without payment, because land is acquired through חֲזָקָה and the money becomes a debt.

rashi.hebrew: חייתא - שק מלא קשרים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines the Aramaic term חייתא as a sack full of knots, clarifying the object of the sale in the Mishnah.

rashi.hebrew: מכי דייש אמצרי - מתקן גבולי השדה ומגביהם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that מכי דייש אמצרי refers to the moment the buyer begins repairing and raising the field's boundaries, marking the start of חֲזָקָה.

steps.title: A dissenting view on liability for defects
steps.stepSummary: Some authorities hold that even if the buyer assumes liability for defects, he can still retract before חֲזָקָה.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a dissenting opinion regarding the effect of assuming liability for defects. This view challenges the implication that assuming liability finalizes the sale.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The dissenting view argues that even if the buyer explicitly assumes liability for defects, he can still retract before חֲזָקָה. This suggests that חֲזָקָה is the only factor that finalizes the sale, not the assumption of liability.
steps.whatToRemember: This view separates the concept of חֲזָקָה from the assumption of liability.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a new topic introduced by the Gemara, not a direct continuation of the previous question.
steps.whyThisMatters: This raises the question of whether חֲזָקָה is the sole determinant of a final sale, or if other factors (like assuming liability) can also finalize it.

phrases.english: And there are those who say, even in a case of 'afterward' also,

phrases.aramaic: דְּאָמַר לֵיהּ: אַחְוִי טִרְפָךְ וַאֲשַׁלֵּם לָךְ.
phrases.english: Who said to him: 'Show me your wound and I will pay you.'

steps.triggerLanguage: וְאִיכָּא דְּאָמְרִי, אֲפִילּוּ בְּאַחְרָיוּת נָמֵי, דְּאָמַר לֵיהּ: אַחְוִי טִרְפָךְ וַאֲשַׁלֵּם לָךְ.

rashi.hebrew: איכא דאמרי אפי' באחריות נמי - אע"ג דסוף סוף עליה הדר אין יכול לחזור בו שהמוכר מעכב עליו ולא מצי אמר הוא הואיל וסופך לשלם לי מעותי השתא דבידי אעכבם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that according to this dissenting view, even if the buyer assumes liability and the money ultimately returns to him, he cannot retract because the seller can withhold the money until the buyer proves the defect in court.

rashi.hebrew: דאמר ליה אחוי טרפך ואשלם לך - כשיעמידוך ויזכו בדין ויכתוב לך הדיין שטר טורף עלי שבדין טרפוה ממך בשבילי בא ואשלם לך וכל כמה דלא עמדת בדין איני ירא מהם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the phrase 'אַחְוִי טִרְפָךְ וַאֲשַׁלֵּם לָךְ' means the buyer promises to pay the seller only after a court officially rules that the animal is a טרפה and issues a document allowing the seller to collect from the buyer.

steps.title: The dispute between Rav and Shmuel on מעות and שבח
steps.stepSummary: The sugya opens with the fundamental dispute between Rav and Shmuel regarding whether a buyer of land that turns out not to belong to the seller retains the principal (מעות) and the improvements (שבח).
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with an איתמר introducing the foundational dispute between Rav and Shmuel on the rights of a buyer whose purchase is invalidated. This sets the stage for the entire sugya.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Rav holds the buyer retains both the principal and the improvements, while Shmuel holds the buyer retains only the principal. This sets the stage for the entire sugya, which will explore the nuances of Shmuel's position and the nature of the payment.
steps.whatToRemember: Rav: מעות יש לו ושבח יש לו. Shmuel: מעות יש לו, שבח אין לו.
steps.confusionAlert: The term שבח here refers to improvements made by the buyer, not just natural appreciation.
steps.whyThisMatters: This is the core halachic question of the sugya: does the buyer get compensated for his investments if the land is taken back?

phrases.aramaic: אִיתְּמַר: הַמּוֹכֵר שָׂדֶה לַחֲבֵירוֹ וְנִמְצֵאת שֶׁאֵינָהּ שֶׁלּוֹ.
phrases.english: It was stated: One who sells a field to his friend and it was found that it…

phrases.aramaic: רַב אָמַר: יֵשׁ לוֹ מָעוֹת וְיֵשׁ לוֹ שֶׁבַח.
phrases.english: Rav says: He has money and he has enhancement.

phrases.aramaic: וּשְׁמוּאֵל אָמַר: מָעוֹת יֵשׁ לוֹ, שֶׁבַח אֵין לוֹ.
phrases.english: And Shmuel says: Money he has, enhancement he does not have.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיתְּמַר: הַמּוֹכֵר שָׂדֶה לַחֲבֵירוֹ וְנִמְצֵאת שֶׁאֵינָהּ שֶׁלּוֹ. רַב אָמַר: יֵשׁ לוֹ מָעוֹת וְיֵשׁ לוֹ שֶׁבַח. וּשְׁמוּאֵל אָמַר: מָעוֹת יֵשׁ לוֹ, שֶׁבַח אֵין לוֹ.

rashi.hebrew: שאינה שלו - שלא היתה של מוכר שהיתה גזולה בידו והנגזל בא ומוציא מיד הלוקח:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the case involves a field that was stolen from its original owner, who now comes to reclaim it from the buyer.

rashi.hebrew: יש לו - ללוקח מן המוכר מעות:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that Rav's position means the buyer can recover the principal amount (מעות) from the seller.

rashi.hebrew: ויש לו שבח - אם זה השביח את הקרקע בזבל או בגדר קודם שהוציא נגזל מידו גובה לוקח מן המוכר שבח ואם תאמר הנגזל יתן השבח שהרי השיבו קרקע משובחת כגון שגזלה משובחת והכסיפה ביד הגזלן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י elaborates that Rav holds the buyer can also recover the value of improvements (שבח) from the seller, even if the stolen field itself was already improved before the theft, addressing a potential objection that the thief should pay for the improvements.

rashi.hebrew: שבח אין לו - לקמן מפרש טעמא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that Shmuel's position that the buyer cannot recover the improvements (שבח) will be explained later in the sugya.

tosafot.hebrew: ויש לו שבח - שהנגזל לוקח הקרקע עם השבח ואינו משלם ללוקח רק היציאה כדין יורד לתוך שדה חבירו שלא ברשות דאם השבח יתר על היציאה נותן לו את היציאה ואותו שבח היתר יקח הלוקח מן הגזלן ומיירי שנגזלה ריקנית:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that according to Rav, the original owner takes the field with its improvements but only pays the buyer for the actual increase in value (יציאה) as per the law of one who enters a field without permission, and the case assumes the field was stolen in an unimproved state.

tosafot.hebrew: ושמואל אמר מעות יש לו - אפילו קנאה שלא באחריות כדפי' לעיל וכן משמע דמיירי דומיא דהכיר בה שאינה שלו דלקמן דמיירי על כרחך שלא באחריות כדקאמר שמואל מעות מתנה דאי באחריות אמאי מעות מתנה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות clarifies that Shmuel's ruling that the buyer recovers the principal (מעות) applies even if the purchase was made without a guarantee (שלא באחריות), as implied by the parallel case discussed later where the seller knew the field wasn't his.

steps.title: The dilemma of פרש לו את השבח
steps.stepSummary: The students ask Rav Huna whether Shmuel's denial of שבח applies even if the seller explicitly agreed to pay for the improvements, or if Shmuel only denies it when no such agreement was made.
steps.whatsHappening: The students raise a שאלה to Rav Huna regarding the scope of Shmuel's ruling. They ask if the denial of שבח is absolute or conditional on the seller not having specified payment for improvements.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The dilemma hinges on the reason for Shmuel's ruling. Is it because the seller didn't agree to pay for improvements, or is it because the transaction looks like a loan since the seller doesn't get the land back? Rav Huna answers that the matter is unclear.
steps.whatToRemember: The dilemma is whether Shmuel's denial of שבח is absolute or conditional on the seller not having specified payment for improvements.
steps.confusionAlert: The term ריבית (interest) is key here. If the seller doesn't get the land back, paying for improvements looks like interest on a loan.
steps.whyThisMatters: This question probes the underlying rationale of Shmuel's position, which will be crucial for understanding the subsequent arguments.

phrases.english: They asked from him, from Rav Huna:
He specified to him the enhancement, what is it?

phrases.aramaic: טַעְמָא דִשְׁמוּאֵל מִשּׁוּם דְּלָא פָּירֵשׁ שְׁבָחָא,
phrases.english: The reason of Shmuel because he did not specify the enhancement,
And here, behold he specified to him,
Or perhaps his reason of Shmuel:

phrases.aramaic: כֵּיוָן דְּלֵית לֵיהּ קַרְקַע, מִחֲזֵי כְּרִיבִּית.
phrases.english: Since he does not have land, it appears as a loan.

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לְהוּ: אִין וְלָאו, וְרַפְיָא בִּידֵיהּ.
phrases.english: He said to them: Yes and no, and it is loose in his hand.
phrases.notes: Rav Huna was undecided on the matter.

steps.triggerLanguage: בְּעוֹ מִינֵּיהּ מֵרַב הוּנָא: פֵּירֵשׁ לוֹ אֶת הַשֶּׁבַח מַהוּ? טַעְמָא דִשְׁמוּאֵל מִשּׁוּם דְּלָא פָּירֵשׁ שְׁבָחָא, וְהָכָא הָא פָּירֵשׁ לֵהּ, אוֹ דִלְמָא טַעְמֵיהּ דִשְׁמוּאֵל: כֵּיוָן דְּלֵית לֵיהּ קַרְקַע, מִחֲזֵי כְּרִיבִּית. אֲמַר לְהוּ: אִין וְלָאו, וְרַפְיָא בִּידֵיהּ.

rashi.hebrew: פירש לו את השבח - כשמכרה לו התנה אם יטרפוה מידך אשלם לך את השבח מהו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the question of פרש לו את השבח refers to a case where the seller explicitly agreed to pay for the improvements if the land were confiscated, asking whether this changes the ruling.

rashi.hebrew: כיון דלית ליה קרקע - הואיל ואין קרקע זו שלו נמצא שאין כאן שום מכר והמעות מלוה אצלו וכשמשלם יותר ממה שנטל מחזי כרבית:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the concern of מחזי כריבית arises because the seller does not retain ownership of the land, making the transaction resemble a loan where paying back more than received constitutes interest.

rashi.hebrew: אין ולאו - תחילה אמר אין וחזר ואמר לאו רפוי היה הדבר בידו שלא היה יודע טעמו של דבר יפה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י interprets Rav Huna's response of אין ולא, ורפיה בידיה as indicating that Rav Huna was uncertain and did not know the precise reason for Shmuel's ruling.

steps.title: Rav Nachman's statement in the name of Shmuel
steps.stepSummary: Rav Nachman states that Shmuel holds the buyer does not get the improvements even if the seller explicitly agreed to pay for them, because the transaction appears to be a loan.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a מימרא from Rav Nachman in the name of Shmuel, clarifying that Shmuel's denial of שבח is absolute, even if the seller specified payment for improvements.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Rav Nachman explains that Shmuel's reason is that since the seller doesn't get the land back, the buyer appears to be standing and taking payment for the use of his money, which is interest. This resolves the previous dilemma by taking the second option.

keyTerms.meaning: Standing and taking payment for the use of his money

steps.whatToRemember: Shmuel's denial of שבח is absolute, even if the seller specified payment for improvements, because it appears to be interest.
steps.confusionAlert: The term שכר מעותיו עומד ונוטל is key here. It means the buyer is effectively charging interest on the money he paid.
steps.whyThisMatters: This statement clarifies Shmuel's position and sets up the subsequent challenge from Rava.

phrases.aramaic: אִיתְּמַר, אָמַר רַב נַחְמָן אָמַר שְׁמוּאֵל:
phrases.english: It was stated, Rav Naḥman says that Shmuel says:
He has the money,
He does not have the enhancement,

phrases.aramaic: אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁפֵּירַשׁ לוֹ אֶת הַשֶּׁבַח.
phrases.english: Even though he specified to him the enhancement.
What is the reason?

phrases.aramaic: כֵּיוָן דְּקַרְקַע אֵין לוֹ, שְׂכַר מְעוֹתָיו עוֹמֵד וְנוֹטֵל.
phrases.english: Since he does not have the land, the wages of his money stand and he takes.
phrases.notes: Refers to the prohibition of interest; the buyer's payment is treated as a loan earning interest.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיתְּמַר, אָמַר רַב נַחְמָן אָמַר שְׁמוּאֵל: מָעוֹת יֵשׁ לוֹ, שֶׁבַח אֵין לוֹ, אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁפֵּירַשׁ לוֹ אֶת הַשֶּׁבַח. מַאי טַעְמָא? כֵּיוָן דְּקַרְקַע אֵין לוֹ, שְׂכַר מְעוֹתָיו עוֹמֵד וְנוֹטֵל.

steps.title: Rava's challenge from the Mishnah in Gittin
steps.stepSummary: Rava challenges Rav Nachman's statement with a Mishnah in Gittin that states one cannot appropriate liened property for improvements, implying that improvements are collected from unsold property.
steps.whatsHappening: Rava brings a קשיא from a Mishnah in Gittin. The Mishnah states that one cannot collect for improvements from liened property, which implies that improvements are collected from unsold property.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Mishnah in Gittin lists cases where one cannot collect from liened property, including for improvements. This implies that for improvements, one collects from unsold property. If the buyer can collect improvements from the seller's unsold property, then Shmuel's denial of שבח seems incorrect.

keyTerms.meaning: For the betterment of the world

steps.whatToRemember: The Mishnah in Gittin states that improvements are collected from unsold property, not liened property.
steps.confusionAlert: The term נכסים משועבדים refers to property that has been sold to pay off debts, while נכסים בני חורין refers to unsold property.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge forces the Gemara to clarify the context of the Mishnah and whether it applies to the case of a buyer whose purchase is invalidated.

phrases.english: Rava raised an objection to Rav Naḥman:

phrases.aramaic: אֵין מוֹצִיאִין לַאֲכִילַת פֵּירוֹת, וְלִשְׁבַח קַרְקָעוֹת,
phrases.english: One does not take out for eating of fruits, and for enhancement of lands,
and for sustenance of the wife and the daughters from liened properties,
because of the betterment of the world.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֵיתִיבֵיהּ רָבָא לְרַב נַחְמָן: אֵין מוֹצִיאִין לַאֲכִילַת פֵּירוֹת, וְלִשְׁבַח קַרְקָעוֹת, וְלִמְזוֹן הָאִשָּׁה וְהַבָּנוֹת מִנְּכָסִים מְשׁוּעְבָּדִים, מִפְּנֵי תִּיקּוּן הָעוֹלָם.

rashi.hebrew: למזון האשה והבנות - תנאי כתובה שמקבל עליו את תהא יתבא בביתי ומיתזנא מנכסי וכן הבנות בנן נוקבן דיהוו ליכי מינאי אינון יהון יתבן בביתי ומיתזנן מנכסי עד דתלקחן לגוברין אין מוציאין לאחד מכל אלו מנכסים משועבדים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Mishnah in גיטין refers to the ketubah obligation for a wife and daughters to be supported from the husband's property, which cannot be collected from נכסים משועבדים.

rashi.hebrew: מפני תיקון העולם - שאם אתה אומר יטרפו לקוחות על כך אין לך אדם שלוקח שדה מחבירו דהא אין לך אדם שאין חוב זה מוטל עליו ודבר שאין לו קצבה היא ואכילת פירות ושבח קרקעות נמי דבר שאינו ידוע הוא ואין הלקוחות יודעין להזהר בכך:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the reason for not collecting these obligations from נכסים משועבדים is תיקון העולם, because these are indefinite obligations that a buyer cannot reasonably anticipate or guard against when purchasing property.

steps.title: The distinction between liened and unsold property
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by distinguishing between liened and unsold property, suggesting that the Mishnah refers to a case where the seller has unsold property from which the buyer can collect improvements.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara offers a תירוץ to Rava's challenge. It suggests that the Mishnah's prohibition on collecting from liened property implies that one can collect from unsold property.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara argues that the Mishnah's prohibition on collecting from liened property implies that one can collect from unsold property. This means that the buyer can collect improvements from the seller's unsold property, which is consistent with Shmuel's position that the buyer doesn't get improvements from the land itself, but can still collect from the seller's other assets.
steps.whatToRemember: The Mishnah's prohibition on collecting from liened property implies that one can collect from unsold property.
steps.confusionAlert: The term נכסים משועבדים refers to property that has been sold to pay off debts, while נכסים בני חורין refers to unsold property.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction is crucial for understanding how the buyer can be compensated for improvements without violating Shmuel's position.

phrases.english: From the enslaved we do not extract,
But from the free – we extract,
And yet, at least for the enhancement of lands,
What? Is it not in a case of one who purchases from a robber!

steps.triggerLanguage: מִמְּשַׁעְבְּדִי הוּא דְּלָא מַפְּקִינַן, הָא מִבְּנֵי (חוֹרִין) – [חָרֵי] מַפְּקִינַן, וְקָתָנֵי מִיהָא לִשְׁבַח קַרְקָעוֹת, מַאי לָאו בְּלוֹקֵחַ מִגַּזְלָן!

rashi.hebrew: מאי לאו בלוקח שדה מגזלן - והשביחה ובא נגזל וטרפה חוזר לוקח וגובה קרן מן המוכר אפילו מנכסים משועבדים שמכר זה אחר מכירה זו דשטרו של זה קודם שכתב בו אחריות והשבח מנכסים בני חורין ולא ממשועבדים מפני תיקון העולם שהשבח לא היה קצוב בשעת מכירה כשקיבל עליו אחריות אלמא אית ליה ללוקח שבח מגזלן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's question assumes the case of a buyer who purchased stolen land and improved it, and when the original owner reclaims the land, the buyer can recover the principal from the thief even from נכסים משועבדים because the thief's document included אחריות, but the buyer can only recover the improvements from נכסים בני חורין due to תיקון העולם, proving that the buyer does have a right to the improvements from the thief.

steps.title: The case of a creditor
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara refines the resolution by suggesting that the Mishnah refers to a case of a creditor, where the seller sold land and the creditor subsequently repossessed it from the buyer.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara offers a more precise תירוץ. It suggests that the Mishnah refers to a case of a creditor, where the seller sold land and the creditor subsequently repossessed it from the buyer.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara argues that the Mishnah refers to a case where the seller sold land and the creditor subsequently repossessed it from the buyer. In this case, the sale was valid, and the seller's payment of improvements does not appear to be interest. This resolves the challenge by distinguishing between a case of theft (where the sale is invalid) and a case of a creditor (where the sale is valid).
steps.whatToRemember: The Mishnah refers to a case of a creditor, where the sale was valid and the seller's payment of improvements does not appear to be interest.
steps.confusionAlert: The term בעל חוב refers to a creditor who has a lien on the seller's property.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction is crucial for understanding how the buyer can be compensated for improvements without violating Shmuel's position.

phrases.english: Not in a creditor.

rashi.hebrew: לא בבעל חוב - שטרפה בעל חוב ממנו בשביל חובו ולא גזולה היתה דההיא ודאי אית ליה שבחא דמכירה מעלייתא היא כל זמן שלא טרפה ממנו שאם פרע המוכר מעותיו לבע"ח הרי הוא מסולק מן הלקוחות הלכך כי משלם ליה יותר ממה שקיבל השתא הוא דהדר זבין מיניה קרקע המכורה לו ולא מחזי כשכר מעותיו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that in a case of a creditor (בעל חוב) who repossessed the land, the sale was valid (מכירה מעלייתא) until the repossession, so if the seller pays the buyer more than the purchase price, it is considered a new sale of the land rather than interest on the money.

steps.title: Challenge from the Mishnah's First Clause
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the creditor resolution by pointing out that the first clause of the Mishnah refers to consuming produce, which a creditor does not have the right to collect.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a קושיא against the creditor resolution. It notes that the first clause of the משנה discusses collecting for consumed produce, which a creditor cannot do.
steps.deeperAnalysis: A creditor only has a lien on the land itself, not on the produce consumed by the buyer. Therefore, the משנה must be discussing a גזלן, who is liable for the produce's value. This directly challenges the previous assumption that the entire משנה refers to a creditor.

phrases.english: If it is a creditor, say the beginning:
One does not appropriate for eating produce,
And if it is a creditor,

phrases.aramaic: בַּעַל חוֹב מִי אִית לֵיהּ פֵּירֵי?
phrases.english: Does a creditor have produce?

phrases.aramaic: וְהָאָמַר שְׁמוּאֵל: בַּעַל חוֹב גּוֹבֶה אֶת הַשֶּׁבַח.
phrases.english: And Shmuel said: A creditor collects the enhancement.
Enhancement – yes, but produce – no!

steps.triggerLanguage: אִי בְּבַעַל חוֹב, אֵימָא רֵישָׁא: אֵין מוֹצִיאִין לַאֲכִילַת פֵּירוֹת, וְאִי בְּבַעַל חוֹב, בַּעַל חוֹב מִי אִית לֵיהּ פֵּירֵי? וְהָאָמַר שְׁמוּאֵל: בַּעַל חוֹב גּוֹבֶה אֶת הַשֶּׁבַח. שֶׁבַח – אִין, אֲבָל פֵּירוֹת – לָא!

rashi.hebrew: לאכילת פירות - ס"ד הא מיירי בשדה שטרפוה מליאה פירות מיד הלוקח וחוזר לוקח לגבות מן המוכר ואי בשטרפה בעל חוב מי אית ליה לבעל חוב פירות גמורין המחוברין בקרקע ואינן צריכין לקרקע כלום שיכול לטורפן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's challenge assumes the Mishnah refers to a field seized full of produce from the buyer, and the buyer seeks to collect from the seller, whereas a creditor cannot seize produce that is still attached to the ground and does not require the land for its sustenance.

rashi.hebrew: גובה את השבח - וכל הקרקע טורף מיד הלוקח כמו שהיא משובחת ע"י הלוקח:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that when שמואל states a creditor collects שבח, it means the creditor can seize the entire land as it is enhanced by the buyer's improvements, but this does not extend to the produce itself.

steps.title: Resolution: The Case of a Robber
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by suggesting that the Mishnah refers to a case of a robber and robbery victim, where the buyer can collect for the produce he consumed.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא offers a תירוץ to resolve the difficulty. It suggests the משנה indeed refers to a גזלן and נגזל, where the robber is liable for the produce.
steps.deeperAnalysis: In a case of theft, the robber is fully liable for the value of the produce consumed. This aligns with the first clause of the משנה. The resolution distinguishes this case from a creditor, who lacks this specific liability.

keyTerms.meaning: A resolution or answer to a difficulty

phrases.english: Rather, it is obvious in a robber and a robbery victim.

phrases.aramaic: וּמִדְּרֵישָׁא בְּגוֹזֵל וְנִגְזָל, סֵיפָא נָמֵי בְּגוֹזֵל וְנִגְזָל.
phrases.english: And from the beginning in a robber and a robbery victim, the end also in a robber…

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא פְּשִׁיטָא בְּגוֹזֵל וְנִגְזָל. וּמִדְּרֵישָׁא בְּגוֹזֵל וְנִגְזָל, סֵיפָא נָמֵי בְּגוֹזֵל וְנִגְזָל.

rashi.hebrew: בגוזל ונגזל - וכגון שגזלה עם פירותיה ומכרה אי נמי גזלה בלא פירות ומכרה לאחר ועשה בה פירות וטוען נגזל ארעאי אשבח ומיהו היציאה שהוציא הלוקח ישלם לו הנגזל שהרי מתוך כך הושבחה יותר ואם אין השבח יותר אלא היציאה יתירה נותן לו יציאה שיעור שבח ודיו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the case of גוזל ונגזל involves a robber who stole property with or without its produce and sold it, where the victim must compensate the buyer for improvements (הוצאה) if they exceed the increase in value (שבח), or pay the full improvement cost if it equals the increase.

tosafot.hebrew: סיפא נמי בגוזל כו' - מרישא עצמה היה יכול להקשות כדמקשה בסמוך אלא כמה שיכול להקשות משבח גופיה פריך:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the Gemara could have challenged the Mishnah from the first clause alone, but instead derives the challenge from the concept of שבח itself to strengthen the difficulty.

steps.title: Rejection: The Cases Are Not Comparable
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara rejects the comparison between the two cases, arguing that they are not comparable and that the Mishnah refers to different cases for each clause.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא rejects the previous resolution with a דחיה. It argues that the two clauses of the משנה do not need to be from the same case.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The principle of הַאי כְּדָאִיתָא וְהַאי כְּדָאִיתָא allows each clause to have its own context. The first clause can refer to a גזלן, while the second clause refers to a creditor. This maintains the distinction between the two scenarios.

keyTerms.meaning: A rejection of a proposed resolution
This case stands as it is, and that case stands as it is

phrases.english: From the creditor?
This one is as it is, and this one is as it is.

steps.triggerLanguage: מִידֵּי אִרְיָא?! הָא כִּדְאִיתָא וְהָא כִּדְאִיתָא.

rashi.hebrew: הא כדאיתא כו' - רישא בנגזל וסיפא בבעל חוב ולקמיה מותיב לה מרישא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's rejection (מידא אריא) means the first clause of the Mishnah refers to a case of גזילה (theft) while the second clause refers to a case of בעל חוב (creditor), and notes that this distinction will be challenged later from the first clause.

steps.title: Challenge from the Baraita
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the rejection by citing a Baraita that elaborates on the Mishnah and seems to contradict the distinction between the two cases.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a new קושיא from a ברייתא. The ברייתא states that improvements are collected from נכסים משועבדים, which contradicts the separation of cases.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If the משנה discusses a creditor, the ברייתa's mention of collecting from נכסים משועבדים seems to imply a single, unified case. This challenges the previous דחיה that the clauses refer to different scenarios.

phrases.aramaic: וְהָא לָא תָּנֵי הָכִי: לְשֶׁבַח קַרְקָעוֹת כֵּיצַד?
phrases.english: And it is not taught so: Regarding enhancement of lands, how?

phrases.aramaic: הֲרֵי שֶׁגָּזַל שָׂדֶה מֵחֲבֵירוֹ וַהֲרֵי הִיא
phrases.english: If one robbed a field from his fellow and behold it
is taken from under his hand,
when he collects –
he collects the principal from liened property,
and the enhancement – he collects from free property.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְהָא לָא תָּנֵי הָכִי: לְשֶׁבַח קַרְקָעוֹת כֵּיצַד? הֲרֵי שֶׁגָּזַל שָׂדֶה מֵחֲבֵירוֹ וַהֲרֵי הִיא יוֹצְאָה מִתַּחַת יָדוֹ, כְּשֶׁהוּא גּוֹבֶה – גּוֹבֶה אֶת הַקֶּרֶן מִנְּכָסִים מְשׁוּעְבָּדִים, וְשֶׁבַח – גּוֹבֶה מִנְּכָסִים בְּנֵי חוֹרִין.

rashi.hebrew: והא לא תני הכי - בברייתא עלה דהך מתניתין דאין מוציאין דתוקמה בבעל חוב:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Baraita cited here is a continuation of the Mishnah, which the Gemara has already established refers to a case of a creditor.

rashi.hebrew: והרי היא יוצאה מתחת ידו - שבית דין מוציאין אותה מידו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the phrase 'יוצאה מתחת ידו' means the court will physically remove the stolen field from the thief's possession.

steps.title: Resolution: The Third-Party Buyer
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by suggesting that the Baraita refers to a case where the robber sold the land to a third party, who then improved it.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא offers a תירוץ involving a third-party לוקח. The ברייתא refers to a case where a גזלן sold land to a third party, who then improved it.
steps.deeperAnalysis: In this scenario, the third-party buyer can collect for improvements from the גזלן's remaining נכסים משועבדים. This resolves the difficulty by showing how a גזלן case can involve נכסים משועבדים.

keyTerms.meaning: A resolution or answer to a difficulty

phrases.aramaic: הֵיכִי דָמֵי? אִילֵּימָא כִּדְקָתָנֵי, גַּזְלָן מִמַּאן גָּבֵי?
phrases.english: How are they similar? If we say as the small ones, from whom does the robber collect?
Rather, is it not:

phrases.aramaic: כְּגוֹן שֶׁגָּזַל שָׂדֶה מֵחֲבֵירוֹ וּמְכָרָהּ לְאַחֵר וְהִשְׁבִּיחָהּ!
phrases.english: Like one who robbed a field from his friend and sold it to another and improved it!

steps.triggerLanguage: הֵיכִי דָמֵי? אִילֵּימָא כִּדְקָתָנֵי, גַּזְלָן מִמַּאן גָּבֵי? אֶלָּא לָאו: כְּגוֹן שֶׁגָּזַל שָׂדֶה מֵחֲבֵירוֹ וּמְכָרָהּ לְאַחֵר וְהִשְׁבִּיחָהּ!

rashi.hebrew: אילימא כדקתני - שלא מכרה גזלן ומידו היא יוצאה בדין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's initial assumption (אילימא כדקתני) is that the robber himself sold the land, meaning the land would be returned directly from his possession through a court ruling.

rashi.hebrew: גזלן ממאן גבי - ממאן יש לו לגבות וכי מה נתן בה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies the Gemara's challenge (גזלן ממאן גבי) by asking from whom the robber can collect payment for the improvements if he is the one who sold the land, implying he gave nothing to the buyer to justify such a claim.

rashi.hebrew: והשביחה - והרי היא בדין יוצאה מתחת ידו של לוקח אלמא אית ליה שבחא ללוקח מגזלן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that in the proposed case where the land is returned from the third-party buyer's possession, the buyer retains the right to collect the value of the improvements (שבחא) from the robber.

rashi.hebrew: שגזל שדה מחבירו ומכרה לאחר והשביח - ונעשה פירות גמורין ונגזל לוקחה עם פירותיה ואינו נותן אלא יציאה דקאמר ארעאי אשבח וקתני דלוקח חוזר וגובה מגזלן מן המחוררין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י details the scenario where the third-party buyer improved the land, the fruits became fully grown, and the land was subsequently stolen from the buyer, establishing that the buyer only loses the land itself but can recover the value of the improvements from the robber's remaining assets.

steps.title: Alternative Resolution: The Creditor Case
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara offers an alternative resolution, suggesting that the Baraita refers to a case of a creditor, where the seller sold land and the creditor subsequently repossessed it from the buyer.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא provides an alternative תירוץ focusing on a creditor. It suggests the ברייתא refers to a case where a creditor repossessed land from a buyer.
steps.deeperAnalysis: In this creditor scenario, the sale was initially valid, and the seller's payment for improvements does not constitute ריבית. This resolves the difficulty by aligning the ברייתא with a creditor context.

keyTerms.meaning: A resolution or answer to a difficulty

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר לָךְ: לָאו תָּרוֹצֵי קָא מְתָרְצַתְּ?
phrases.english: He said to you: Are you not resolving?
Resolve also with regard to a creditor.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר לָךְ: לָאו תָּרוֹצֵי קָא מְתָרְצַתְּ? תָּרֵיץ נָמֵי בְּבַעַל חוֹב.

tosafot.hebrew: תריץ נמי בבעל חוב - בפרק מי שמת (ב'ב דף קנז: ושם ד"ה גובה) רצה להוכיח דאקני קנה ומכר משתעבד מהכא דגבי בעל חוב שבח שלא היה בעין בשעת הלואה ודחי ר"מ היא דאמר אדם מקנה דבר שלא בא לעולם וא"ת ולוכח משמואל דאמר בעל חוב גובה את השבח ושמואל גופיה הוא דבעי ליה התם דלא מצי למימר דשמואל נמי סבר כר"מ ומבעי ליה אליבא דרבנן דהא בפרק אע"פ (כתובות דף נט.) פסק שמואל כר' יוחנן הסנדלר דלית ליה אדם מקנה כו' וי"ל דהא דאמר שמואל בעל חוב גובה את השבח היינו לפי המסקנא דהתם דפשיט דאקני קנה ומכר משתעבד אף לרבנן ואם תאמר אדרבה לוכח מהכא דלא משתעבד מדלא טריף בע"ח ממשעבדי דטריף לוקח דאין לומר דאחר שטרף בע"ח קרקע מן הלוקח קנה המוכר קרקעות ומכרן דא"כ גם השבח יגבה מהנהו משעבדי דגם השבח היה קצוב כיון שכבר טרף הבעל חוב קודם שלקח זה הקרקע וי"ל דמיירי שזה הקרקע עשאו המוכר לבעל חוב אפותיקי ולכך גובה אותו בע"ח ואע"פ שיש שאר משעבדי אך קשה אמאי דחיק לדחויי בפרק מי שמת (ב"ב דף קנז: ושם) הא מני ר"מ היא יעמיד כשעשאו אפותיקי דאז בעל חוב גובה את השבח היתר על היציאה חנם כדין יורד לתוך שדה חבירו כדמשמע לקמן או יעמיד בלוקח מן הגזלן וכגון שיש לו קרקע או קנו מידו כדמתרץ לקמן בשמעתין:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the Gemara's suggestion to resolve the case as a creditor (בעל חוב) is necessary to prove that the obligation of a creditor is derived from the same source as קנין, as discussed in בבא בתרא קנז:.

steps.title: Challenge from the Baraita on Produce
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the creditor resolution by citing a Baraita that elaborates on the Mishnah and seems to contradict the distinction between the two cases.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a קושיא against the creditor resolution. It cites a ברייתא stating that produce is collected from נכסים משועבדים, which contradicts the creditor model.
steps.deeperAnalysis: A creditor cannot collect for consumed produce from נכסים משועבדים. This challenges the previous תירוץ and suggests the משנה must refer to a single case, likely involving a גזלן.

phrases.english: Come and hear: Regarding eating of produce, how?

phrases.aramaic: הֲרֵי שֶׁגָּזַל שָׂדֶה מֵחֲבֵירוֹ וַהֲרֵי הִיא
phrases.english: If he robbed a field from his friend and behold it
goes out from under his hand,
when he collects –
he collects the principal from encumbered property,
and produce he collects from free property.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּא שְׁמַע: לַאֲכִילַת פֵּירוֹת כֵּיצַד? הֲרֵי שֶׁגָּזַל שָׂדֶה מֵחֲבֵירוֹ וַהֲרֵי הִיא יוֹצְאָה מִתַּחַת יָדוֹ, כְּשֶׁהוּא גּוֹבֶה – גּוֹבֶה אֶת הַקֶּרֶן מִנְּכָסִים מְשׁוּעְבָּדִים, וּפֵירוֹת גּוֹבֶה מִנְּכָסִים בְּנֵי חוֹרִין.

steps.title: Resolution: The Third-Party Buyer
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by suggesting that the Baraita refers to a case where the robber sold the land to a third party, who then improved it.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא offers a תירוץ involving a third-party לוקח. The ברייתא refers to a case where a גזלן sold land to a third party, who then improved it.
steps.deeperAnalysis: In this scenario, the third-party buyer can collect for improvements from the גזלן's remaining נכסים משועבדים. This resolves the difficulty by showing how a גזלן case can involve נכסים משועבדים.

keyTerms.meaning: A resolution or answer to a difficulty

phrases.aramaic: הֵיכִי דָמֵי? אִילֵּימָא כִּדְקָתָנֵי, גַּזְלָן מִמַּאן גָּבֵי?
phrases.english: How are they similar? If we say as the small ones, from whom does the robber collect?
Rather, is it not:

phrases.aramaic: כְּגוֹן שֶׁגָּזַל שָׂדֶה מֵחֲבֵירוֹ וּמְכָרָהּ לְאַחֵר וְהִשְׁבִּיחָהּ!
phrases.english: Like one who robbed a field from his friend and sold it to another and improved it!

steps.triggerLanguage: הֵיכִי דָמֵי? אִילֵּימָא כִּדְקָתָנֵי, גַּזְלָן מִמַּאן גָּבֵי? אֶלָּא לָאו: כְּגוֹן שֶׁגָּזַל שָׂדֶה מֵחֲבֵירוֹ וּמְכָרָהּ לְאַחֵר וְהִשְׁבִּיחָהּ!

steps.title: רַבָּא's Explanation of the Baraita
steps.stepSummary: Rava explains that the Baraita refers to a case where the robber stole a field full of produce, consumed the produce, and damaged the field by digging pits, ditches, and caves.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבָּא offers a resolution to the baraita. He posits a scenario where the גַּזְלָן consumed the produce and damaged the land.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבָּא distinguishes between the principal debt and the value of the produce. This allows the נִגְזָל to collect the principal from נְכָסִים מְשׁוּעְבָּדִים and the produce value from נְכָסִים בְּנֵי חוֹרִין.

phrases.english: Rava said: With what are we dealing here?
Such as one who robbed a field from his fellow,
full of produce, and he ate the produce,
and he dug in it pits, ditches, and caves.
The robbed one came to collect the principal –
he collects from liened property,
The robbed one came to collect the produce –
he collects from unsold property.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רָבָא: הָכָא בְּמַאי עָסְקִינַן – כְּגוֹן שֶׁגָּזַל שָׂדֶה מֵחֲבֵירוֹ, מְלֵאָה פֵּירוֹת, וְאָכַל אֶת הַפֵּירוֹת, וְחָפַר בָּהּ בּוֹרוֹת, שִׁיחִין וּמְעָרוֹת. בָּא נִגְזָל לִגְבּוֹת קֶרֶן – גּוֹבֶה מִנְּכָסִים מְשׁוּעְבָּדִים, בָּא נִגְזָל לִגְבּוֹת פֵּירוֹת – גּוֹבֶה מִנְּכָסִים בְּנֵי חוֹרִין.

rashi.hebrew: אמר רבא כו' - האי כשהוא גובה לאו בלוקח קאמר דבשלא מכרה עסקינן וגובה את הקרן דקאמר בנגזל וא"ת קרן אמאי גובה מנכסים משועבדים הרי קרקע לפניו כגון שחפר בה בורות דאפחתה משוייה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that when the Baraita states the victim collects the קרן, it refers to the victim himself collecting from the robber's נכסים משועבדים, not a purchaser, because the case assumes the robber did not sell the field, and the victim can collect the principal from liened property because the robber diminished the field's value by digging pits, ditches, and caves.

steps.title: רַבָּה בַּר רַב הוּנָא's Explanation of the Baraita
steps.stepSummary: Rabba bar Rav Huna explains that the Baraita refers to a case where gentile thugs took the field from the robber by force.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבָּה בַּר רַב הוּנָא offers an alternative resolution. He suggests the land was taken by מְסִיקִין.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This scenario changes the nature of the loss. The נִגְזָל can still collect the principal from נְכָסִים מְשׁוּעְבָּדִים and the produce value from נְכָסִים בְּנֵי חוֹרִין.

phrases.english: Rabba bar Rav Huna said:
Like when they took it from the masikin,
phrases.notes: Masikin refers to those who have no fixed residence or assets.

phrases.english: the robbed one came to collect the principal –
he collects from encumbered property,
the robbed one came to collect the produce –
he collects from free property.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַבָּה בַּר רַב הוּנָא אָמַר: כְּגוֹן שֶׁנְּטָלוּהָ מַסִּיקִין, בָּא נִגְזָל לִגְבּוֹת קֶרֶן – גּוֹבֶה מִנְּכָסִים מְשׁוּעְבָּדִים, בָּא נִגְזָל לִגְבּוֹת פֵּירוֹת – גּוֹבֶה מִנְּכָסִים בְּנֵי חוֹרִין.
